text: The surgeon makes an incision in the left upper quadrant at Palmer's point and accesses the abdomen using the OptiView technique, preparing for the laparoscopic appendectomy.
The surgeon places two additional trocars, one in the left lower quadrant and another in the suprapubic position, to facilitate the laparoscopic appendectomy.
The surgeon examines the right lower quadrant, identifying key anatomical structures relevant to the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the appendix, terminal ileum, and cecum within the surgical field before proceeding with dissection.
The surgeon begins the laparoscopic appendectomy by carefully tearing the peritoneum overlying the base of the appendix, exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector and a bowel grasper to create an opening at the base of the appendix, following the initial incision and trocar placement.
The surgeon mobilizes the appendix from its lateral attachments, freeing it from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to dissect tissue around the appendix, providing precise cutting and coagulation in the surgical field.
The surgeon considers two options: dividing the base of the appendix from the cecum or dividing the mesentery of the appendix, with both structures visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the mesentery of the appendix, which shows minimal inflammation, using surgical instruments.
The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to divide the mesentery of the appendix, ensuring hemostasis from the base to the tip of the appendix.
The surgeon inserts a surgical stapler through the left lower quadrant port, preparing to divide the base of the appendix from the cecum.
The surgeon positions the stapler in the left lower quadrant port, allowing for increased mobility and articulation during the stapling of the appendix base.
The surgeon prepares to use a medium thickness stapler to divide the base of the appendix from the cecum, following the division of the mesentery.
The surgeon positions the stapler to include a small portion of the cecal cap with each specimen, a routine step to prevent stump appendicitis.
The surgeon visually confirms the anatomical structures surrounding the appendix before firing the stapler, ensuring accurate placement and avoiding complications.
The surgeon places the divided appendix into an endocatch bag and removes it from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon inspects the cecum and appendiceal mesentery to confirm hemostasis, followed by irrigation of the pelvis and removal of any residual fluid.
The surgeon closes all laparoscopic ports, completing the appendectomy procedure.
